deductions.reason: Across all three transcripts, the tutor never explicitly explains why it is guiding rather than giving answers—i.e., never articulates that the Socratic approach builds retention, active reasoning, or long-term skill. The tutor enforces constraints (e.g., 'No, I'm not going to write a bullet for you to copy') but does not explain the pedagogical rationale behind those constraints.

In Transcripts 2 and 3, after students produce noticeably improved drafts following earlier confusion, the tutor moves directly to critique and next steps without prompting reflection on what changed in the student's thinking or why the improved approach worked better.

In Transcript 2, Turn 9, the tutor says 'Using my own judgment, not your instructor's rubric: your structure works'—while this attempts to clarify formative framing, the phrasing 'your structure works' combined with the explicit disclaimer could still read as quasi-evaluative judgment. However, this is borderline; the tutor does attempt to signal it is not official grading. Deduction applied with low confidence given ambiguity.

grade.overview: Across all three transcripts, the tutor demonstrates strong Socratic discipline—consistently refusing to give direct answers, redirecting students to their own reasoning, and scaffolding progressively from vague to specific. No submission-ready content was produced. The main weaknesses are: (1) the tutoring rationale is never explicitly stated (why guided discovery rather than direct answers), and (2) after students make clear improvements, the tutor does not pause to prompt reflection on what changed. The 3.2.B.b deduction in Transcript 2 is applied with low confidence—the tutor's disclaimer is an attempt at formative framing, but the phrasing edges toward evaluative judgment.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never produces submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and consistently keeps key idea generation student-led across all three conversations. 1.2 receives full marks: the tutor builds on student progress at each turn and uses diagnostic questioning before responding. 1.3 loses 4 points: the tutoring rationale (why Socratic method supports learning) is never articulated in any transcript (1.3.A.a, -2), and after students produce improved drafts in T2 and T3, the tutor moves to critique without prompting reflection on what changed (1.3.C.a, -2). Dialogue quality is clean across all transcripts—no spiraling, no assignment drift, no off-topic engagement. Communication is generally bite-sized and clear; the 3.2.B.b deduction reflects the borderline evaluative phrasing in T2 Turn 9, though the tutor does attempt a disclaimer.
